plainMeaning: So, at all these wonder-filled holy places like Aṣṭāpada, bow to the moon-bright Jinas, before whom even the kings of the gods bow with heartfelt devotion.
recitation: iya bahuviha acchheraya nihīsu aṭṭhāvayāi ṭhāṇesu | paṇamaha jiṇavara-cande, subhatti-bhara namira-māhinde ||

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit for Skt. itthaṁ / iti; a summarising deictic 'thus', here pointing back over the just-narrated tīrthas.
wordByWord.notes: Marks the verse as a recapitulation, not a new site.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit for Skt. āścarya 'wonder, marvel'; doubled conjunct preserved verbatim from the book.
wordByWord.notes: Refers collectively to the miraculous events told of the preceding tīrthas.

wordByWord.gloss: in the treasure-troves, in the treasuries
wordByWord.etymology: Locative plural of nihi = Skt. nidhi 'treasure, hoard' (nidhiṣu).
wordByWord.notes: The holy sites are figured as treasuries of marvels; reads in parallel with ṭhāṇesu.

wordByWord.gloss: Aṣṭāpada and the rest (places beginning with Aṣṭāpada)
wordByWord.etymology: aṭṭhāvaya = Skt. Aṣṭāpada (the mountain of Ṛṣabha's nirvāṇa) + -āi (Skt. ādi 'and the rest').
wordByWord.notes: Naming Aṣṭāpada — the first tīrtha narrated (vv.41–45) — rings the legend-section shut; the ādi gathers all the other sites under one head.

wordByWord.gloss: in the places, at the sites
wordByWord.etymology: Locative plural of ṭhāṇa = Skt. sthāna 'place, holy site' (sthāneṣu).
Imperative 2nd person plural of paṇam- = Skt. pra-√nam, praṇamata 'do bow'.
wordByWord.notes: The verse pivots from narration to direct command of the assembly.

wordByWord.gloss: the supreme Jina, best of Jinas
wordByWord.notes: Forms one unit with cande: jiṇavara-cande.

wordByWord.etymology: Accusative plural of canda = Skt. candra 'moon'; jiṇavara-canda = 'moon among the supreme Jinas'.
wordByWord.notes: Applies the hymn's stock moon-image (cool, luminous) to the enshrined Jinas of all these sites; object of paṇamaha.
Signals bhāva-worship rightly directed, not boon-seeking.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. bhara 'load, fullness'; subhatti-bhara = 'a flood/abundance of pure devotion'.

wordByWord.gloss: bowing, prone to homage, habitually bowing
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit habitual-agent adjective in -ira from √nam 'to bow' (cf. hasira 'smiling', gamira 'going'); denotes a constant disposition.
wordByWord.notes: The -ira suffix makes the great Indras' bowing their settled, ceaseless character — not a one-time act.

wordByWord.etymology: māhinda = Skt. mahendra (mahā 'great' + indra), the chief lords of the heavens.
wordByWord.notes: Construed with namira: 'bowed to by the bowing great Indras'; the kings of the gods themselves do homage at these shrines.

literalTranslation: Thus, in [these] treasuries of manifold wonders, in places such as Aṣṭāpada — bow ye to the moons-among-the-supreme-Jinas, [who are] saluted by the ever-bowing great Indras with a flood of pure devotion.
idiomaticTranslation: And so, across these treasure-houses of countless marvels — Aṣṭāpada and every such holy site — bow down, O devotees, to the moon-bright supreme Jinas, before whom even the mighty Indras bend low again and again, their hearts brimming with pure devotion.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This is the seal of the great legend-cycle (vv.41–69). The opening 'iya' (thus) sweeps the hand back over everything just told — Aṣṭāpada, Girnar, Daśārṇakūṭa, Takṣaśilā, Ahicchatra, Rathāvarta/Kuñjarāvarta and Camarutpāta at Susumārapura — and renames them all at a stroke as 'treasuries of manifold wonders' (bahuviha acchheraya nihīsu). By naming Aṣṭāpada (aṭṭhāvayāi) the poet closes a ring: Aṣṭāpada was the very first narrated tīrtha (vv.41–45), so to fold the section shut by re-invoking it frames the whole cycle; the suffix -āi ('and the rest') economically gathers every other site into the single locative ṭhāṇesu. The imperative 'paṇamaha' then turns the poem from story-telling to direct address — the narration stops and the assembly is commanded to bow. The object, jiṇavara-cande, casts the enshrined Jinas of all these places as 'moons among Jinas', cool and luminous. The clinching stroke is namira-māhinde: even the mahendras, the great Indras who rule the heavens, are 'ever-bowing' — the habitual -ira suffix makes homage their permanent disposition — and they bow subhatti-bhara, awash in pure devotion. The rhetorical force is a fortiori: if the very kings of the gods bow ceaselessly and wholeheartedly here, how much more should the human reciter. The verse thus converts a catalogue of marvels into an imperative of humility, handing the worshipper off to the geographic roll-call that opens at the next verse (Sammeta Śikhara).
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Jain conviction that the Jina — utterly detached, beyond any need of worship — is nonetheless the supreme object of veneration for every being, the devas included. The image of Indra in ceaseless homage is the same conviction enshrined in the Śakra-stava tradition: even celestial sovereignty is lesser than the dispassionate, victorious Jina. The word subhatti (pure devotion) marks the bhakti the verse prizes as rightly directed — recognition of the Jina's qualities, the bhāva-worship that ripens toward liberation, not petition for boons. Reframing the pilgrimage marvels as 'treasuries of wonders' subordinates the spectacular to the venerable: the wonders are pointers, the bowing is the point.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: At this hinge the reciter stops 'visiting' sites in imagination and performs the very gesture the hymn keeps modelling — the bow. Holding the picture of Indra bowing wholeheartedly lets one measure and correct one's own half-hearted reverence: the verse asks not for more marvels witnessed but for deeper bhāva in the bow being made right now.

crossReferences.text: Śakrastava (Namuthuṇaṁ) — Indra's homage to the Jina; Nava Smaraṇa no. 7
crossReferences.source: Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed., Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (smaran-7-shakrastava)

crossReferences.text: Logassa (Caturviṁśati-stava) — salutation of the 24 supreme Jinas (jiṇavara)
crossReferences.source: Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed., Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (logassa)

crossReferences.text: Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā vv.41–45 — the Aṣṭāpada / Ṛṣabha-nirvāṇa passage to which this verse rings back

sources.title: Apte, V. S., The Practical Sanskrit-English Dictionary (1890), Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon
sources.consultedFor: Roots and senses of āścarya (acchheraya), nidhi (nihi), sthāna (ṭhāṇa), mahendra (māhinda) and pra-√nam (paṇamaha)

sources.title: Monier-Williams, A Sanskrit-English Dictionary (1899), Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon
sources.consultedFor: Confirming bahuvidha, bhara, su-bhakti and candra; cross-checking the mahendra and jina-vara compounds

sources.title: Ashtapada (Wikipedia)
sources.consultedFor: Identification of Aṣṭāpada (aṭṭhāvaya) as the mountain associated with Ṛṣabhadeva's nirvāṇa, the site this verse rings back to

notes: Verse-specific caveats: (1) The second line admits two readings of namira-māhinde that converge in sense — (a) instrumental/bahuvrīhi, 'bow to the Jinas who are saluted by the ever-bowing great Indras', and (b) comparative-appositional, 'bow... as the devotion-flooded, ever-bowing Mahendras do'. The translation foregrounds (a) and notes (b). (2) aṭṭhāvayāi is parsed as Aṣṭāpada + ādi ('and the rest'), making the line a summarising locative over the whole narrated cycle rather than a claim about Aṣṭāpada alone; an alternative parse reads -āi as a plain plural/oblique ending, with the same recapitulatory force. (3) nihīsu is taken as locative plural of nidhi ('treasuries'); some editions may gloss it as 'abodes'. (4) Orthography (doubled conjuncts in અચ્છેરય, અઠ્ઠાવયાઇ; anusvāras) is reproduced verbatim per the book's fidelity rule and not normalised. (5) The placement of this verse as the closing salutation of vv.41–69, immediately before the Sammeta Śikhara roll-call, follows the preface's structure note and the supplied preceding/following verses.
